summary: Gruber highlights the iPad coding app Codea's innovative approach to bringing menu bars to iOS. The app's creators realized that menus — a decades-old solved problem in desktop computing — were exactly what their complex app needed on iOS. Rather than simply porting the Mac menu bar, Codea designed a touch-native take on menus that looks and feels like it belongs on iOS. Gruber argues this kind of interface element is desperately needed as a standard component on iPad and could work on iPhone too. He connects this to his earlier argument that the menu bar is a vastly underestimated foundation of desktop computing that iOS still lacks.
key_points: Codea's developers spent over a year stuck on design for a universal version before realizing menus — solved decades ago on desktop — were the answer
The implementation isn't a Mac menu bar port but a touch-native, iOS-looking take on menus informed by what works on touchscreens
iPad desperately needs something like this as a standard interface element, not just a third-party solution
iOS remains an 11-year-old platform still floundering to establish consistent conventions for features that are simple on Mac
Gruber uses the aside to criticize iOS 7-12's nearly shadowless iPad popovers as looking like rendering bugs
money_quotes: I realised six months ago as I was using my Mac, using the menus, that I need these things — menus — in Codea. I was trying to solve a problem that has been solved for decades.
The menu bar has become a vastly underestimated foundation of desktop computing. Once heralded, the menu bar is now seen as a vestige.
Without one, iOS is an 11-year-old platform that is still floundering to establish consistent conventions for some basic features, let alone complex ones, that are simple and obvious on the Mac.

What they're doing here with Codea isn't just putting the Mac menu bar on iOS. They've designed and built a very iOS-looking take on a menu bar, deeply informed by the aspects of the Mac menu bar that do work on a touch screen.
Something like this is desperately needed as a standard interface element on iPad, and I think could work on iPhone too.
Ever since iOS 7 I've thought iPad popovers look like a rendering bug or an early prototype.
key_insight: The menu bar is a solved design pattern that iOS desperately needs to adapt rather than ignore, and Codea demonstrates that a touch-native take on menus is both possible and effective.
